title: Rug backing and finishing techniques
subtitle: How to line, bind, and back a hand-worked rug to protect and secure it
excerpt: The finishing stage that turns a worked rug face into a usable floor covering. Covers binding the edge with rug binding tape, applying a non-slip mesh backing, adding a woven or canvas lining, and blocking the finished piece. Also covers storage, hand-washing, and when to use a rug gripper versus a full backing lining.
sourceNotes:
- Lynne Stein, Rugmaking: The Complete Guide: binding, backing, and lining techniques
- Jane Redman, Frame Loom Weaving: blocking and stretching hand-worked textiles
- General rug-making practice: non-slip mesh backing application, rug gripper vs. canvas lining

glossaryTerms.definition: A sturdy woven tape (usually cotton or jute) sewn or glued around the edge of a rug to cover raw or vulnerable edges and prevent fraying.
Open-weave jute fabric used as a stable base layer for hooked, punched, and latch-hook rugs; also used as a lining on the reverse of finished rugs.
Wetting, stretching, and pinning a textile to its planned dimensions and leaving it to dry in shape; corrects distortion and evens out the surface.
An open latex or rubberised mesh applied to the underside of a rug to prevent sliding on hard floors.
A fabric layer attached to the back of a finished rug to protect the worked surface, conceal the backing, and add weight and durability.

content.text: A hand-worked rug that is not properly finished will fray at the edges, skid on the floor, and wear unevenly in use. Good finishing adds 1-3 hours to a project but extends the life of the rug significantly. The three main finishing stages are: edge binding, backing, and
blocking
. Not every rug needs all three; the section on each technique below explains when it applies.
Edge binding
Binding the edge with
rug binding tape
is the standard edge finish for hooked, latch-hook, and needlepoint rugs. The tape folds over the edge, covering raw hessian or canvas on both faces.
Step 1: Trim any excess backing fabric to within 1-2 cm of the worked area. Fold the excess over to the back of the rug and press flat (or use a few tacking stitches to hold it in place).
Step 2: Cut binding tape to the perimeter length of the rug plus 5 cm for mitred corners. Starting at one corner, align the centre of the tape with the rug edge.

Step 3: Whip-stitch or machine-sew the tape along the front edge of the rug using a strong upholstery thread. Work corners by mitreing the tape at a 45-degree fold; a few diagonal stitches through the fold secure the mitre.
Step 4: Fold the tape over to the back of the rug, pulling firmly so no tape is visible from the front, and whip-stitch the back edge down. Use small, tight stitches spaced 5-8 mm apart.
Backing: non-slip mesh
A
non-slip backing
is suitable for lightweight rugs (braided, woven, or macramé) that do not require a structural lining. It can also be used under heavier rugs as an alternative to adhesive gripper tape.

Step 1: Cut non-slip mesh (available as rug gripper roll from hardware or flooring suppliers) to the same dimensions as the finished rug minus 2 cm on all sides. Cutting it slightly smaller than the rug prevents the mesh from showing at the edges.

Step 2: Lay the rug face-down on a clean surface. Place the mesh on the back. Attach with 4-6 large whip stitches at the corners and mid-points through the mesh openings into the rug backing; or use fabric adhesive spray for a flat, stitch-free application.
Backing: canvas or hessian lining
A full canvas or
hessian backing
lining

is suited to heavier rugs (hooked, latch-hook, or hand-knotted) where the worked back is rough or uneven. The lining protects the floor surface, conceals the knots or loops on the back, and adds weight for better drape.
Step 1: Cut the lining fabric 3 cm larger than the rug on all sides. Fold and press a 1.5 cm hem on all sides of the lining so the folded edge matches the rug dimensions.
Step 2: Lay the lining on the back of the rug, folded hems inward. Pin around the perimeter.

Step 3: Slip-stitch the lining to the rug binding tape or the folded backing edge using upholstery thread. Work corners with a neat mitre fold. Do not sew through to the front of the rug; the stitches should catch only the binding tape or backing layer.
Blocking and stretching

Blocking is essential for woven rugs and recommended for any hand-worked rug that has come off the frame with uneven edges or a slight curl. Wet the rug thoroughly (a garden hose or a bathtub soak), press firmly between towels to remove excess water, then lay flat on a clean surface. Using a tape measure, stretch to planned dimensions and pin or staple the edges to a flat board or frame, working outward from the centre on each side. Leave to dry completely (at least 24 hours; thicker rugs may need 2-3 days). Remove pins only after the rug is fully dry.
Storage and washing

To store a finished rug: roll face-out around a cardboard tube (do not fold, which can create permanent creases in the worked surface). Wrap in clean cotton cloth or acid-free tissue and store flat or on end in a cool, dry location.

To hand-wash: lay the rug flat in a bathtub or on a clean waterproof surface. Apply a small amount of pH-neutral wool wash or rug shampoo with a soft brush; work in the direction of the pile, not against it. Rinse thoroughly until no suds remain. Press to remove water; do not wring or agitate. Lay flat to dry as above.
Troubleshooter

attrs.problem: Binding tape puckers or lifts at the corners

content.text: The corner mitre was not cut cleanly or was not stitched through the fold. Re-mitre by trimming the excess tape at the corner to a true 45-degree angle and sewing a diagonal stitch through the fold before continuing along the next edge.

attrs.problem: Rug curls at the edges after washing

content.text: The rug was dried standing up or was not blocked flat. Always dry a washed rug flat and stretched. Repeat the blocking process: wet, stretch to dimensions, pin, dry fully.

attrs.problem: Non-slip mesh detaches from the backing after use

content.text: Spray adhesive is not durable under repeated washing. For a more permanent fix, attach the mesh with evenly spaced whip stitches through the mesh openings every 10-15 cm across the full area, not just at the corners.
